speaker: interviewer
text: September 3, 1996, survivor, Sy Sterns. Interviewer, Phyllis Dreazen. Northbrook, Illinois, USA. Language of the interview is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Phyllis Dreazen. I'm interviewing Sy Sterns in Northbrook, Illinois. It's September 3, 1996. We'll be speaking in English. What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sy Sterns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that please.

speaker: interviewee
text: First name is S-Y S-E-T-E-R-N-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sterns, S-T-E-R--

speaker: interviewee
text: E-R-N-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: At birth was the same name. My name is the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it spelled the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schsan. Sternschsan.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, would you spell that for us, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: S-T-E-R-N-S- -- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] C-H-S-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Simcha.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were called to the Torah, you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Simcha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Simcha Ben--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ben Yaakov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ben Yaakov. Did you ever have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I had another name, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Shlomo Simon Mastowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you have that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: That name I had for a period from 1946 till-- till I got citizenship was 1955. Yeah, became a United States citizen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the circumstances that you had that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The circumstances-- when we went in Czechoslovakia to the border, we had to take a different name because the camp that we were in Bad Reichenhall, they shipped the younger people-- they went to Israel. And the Germans wouldn't have-- they don't want no more PD, displaced person. So we took their identity. And everybody was getting a-- from UNRRA a package. And they will-- they were coming for them-- we were getting the packages. And this name stayed with it. There's a lot of people like that. I had for the camp, had it, that name. Because then all of them people, when they left for Israel, they replaced them with younger people. And the older people stayed there for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet this Shaolin Mastowitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is in Israel. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: You never came across him in later years?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born? What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: You'll be 74.

speaker: interviewee
text: 74. But I'm just 73 till October, eh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Mzhirech. M-Z-H-I-R-E-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in-- by [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about that town.

speaker: interviewee
text: The town was a small town. And all the Jewish people lived in the center of the city. And the Gentiles lived in, like, in the suburbs, and along the outskirts of the city. And as far-- all the business people were Jewish, and the Gentiles were farmers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jewish families, about, were in this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would think about 2,000 families. What are families? What do we consider? Two people, three, or four. You know, there's a big difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you like to consider it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. An average family was four people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Parents--two parents and children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Four people in average. At least four. Some had five.

speaker: interviewer
text: What percentage of the town was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I say, the-- this-- I would say about 80%. And I think from the 80%, and when the war was over, 150 people was alive. And 50 of them were the people where they went in the woods, that part of them. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your town was a town that was famous for Hasidim.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family Hasidish also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We were freemen but not Hasidim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our family was three children. I was the middle child. And my brother was Yitzchok and Simcha and my sister was Sosie.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents' name was Yakov Ben Elihezer Sternsczs. And my mother was Rifke Bat Itzhak-- Bat Itzhak. And she was born in Brezhnev. My father was born in Miedzyrzecz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they happen to meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: A shadkhan. I remember they tell me. I think my father was older. And there was a shadkhan, and they matched them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a good match?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- yeah, I guess so. Yeah. I think maybe they were not poor people so-- and my mother was [INAUDIBLE] she come from-- not from a poor family either. So it just worked out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are your memories of a happy family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they like each other, your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, most of the time. It's like every other family sometime. They had certain disagreements too, though. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But most of the time was-- was pretty good. In Europe, they didn't know nothing. They only know how to work, and go to shul, and no vacation, no goynish. That's it. Yontif you got, you know, but I mean, they was rarely observing, like, all of the Jewish holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother cover her hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only when she bentsh licht. I mean, for Shabbos or a yontif. But she didn't always, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a mikveh in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, the temple-- not every temple had mikvehs though.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother go to a mikveh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-uh. Usually most of the time the men did go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I remember I was younger, you know. And I did go in the mikveh. But I-- I never hear that about-- so they said, maybe there were some, but not too many women need to go to the mikveh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or maybe they went, and they didn't tell you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe you're right. But I know my mother didn't go. I didn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have a beard?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was clean-shaven.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he had that little mustache, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have payos?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No payos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he wear a hat during the day or a kippa?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, didn't-- when he were in the business, he didn't wear no hat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: On Shabbos when we go-- when we eat a meal, he wear a yarmulke and make a bracha and then we seated. And Friday night, the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he didn't wear a kapota or a shtreimel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my-- my-- my zayde did. He did wear a kapota. He was more-- he was-- he was more-- because the business-- it was my zayde's business. And he had two sons. And one-- the other son was in Russia. So the two-- the business left by him for my father and Tzike. And he got kind of older. And he said he don't want to-- He used to come still there, but he was not too active.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then, yeah, then the brothers had kind of an argument. And they split up. He had to pay him out so much. And he had-- it's in the same business but maybe-- and let's see. How many? Maybe about 10 businesses, I think he opened up. And they were-- he was successful too. What kind of business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hardware business. I mean, we had the hardware business, and then we had the factory that was making farm machinery. And after that they made the machine shop near the house. And I think it was a half a block. He was then working. He had to machine it and then assemble it. And they'd be gone for a long time, but I think towards the end, I think we liquidated. There wasn't enough profit in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your zayde, was he--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born there too, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he and your father get along, or did he feel that your father should be more religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't-- he didn't pursue that. He was very religious. He used to go and-- every day, three times a day he used to go davening. And then they used the Talmud. They read and learned in that. I remember one thing they used to check me if I have the [NON-ENGLISH] tzitzit. And I seen they are on this side, I run across the street. But they see me, say, come over, come over. What happened to the tzitze. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I forgot it.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHING] OK, next time you wear the-- wear the [NON-ENGLISH] you know the [NON-ENGLISH] I wear the tzitzit. But, I mean, sometime, you know, when you play ball, it's just too warm to wear it though. You're a little kid, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you supposed to wear a yarmulke too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never wore a yarmulke. I wore it going to shul, yeah. I used to go to every Friday and Saturday night. No, Friday night and Saturday. Not Saturday night. And a lot of times, we used to bring in a guest just about quite a few times, like Friday night, and Shabbos-- and then-- yeah, for Shabbos. But they didn't sleep at our store. I think they slept in the temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a big mitzvah to bring somebody for a meal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sometimes, like, it's a couple of times we had even two people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: The house. The house was a house with a kitchen, and a living room, and a dining room and two bedrooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was considered a big house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was a big house. And I remember one time I had washed the floor, and I slipped on the-- I was-- I was skiing like, you know, it's wet. And I broke my ankle. And I had to have this tire iron by the bed. You know, they have weight. And I had to stay laying in the bed. I remember like no, oh, my gosh-- at least six weeks in the bed. And you couldn't, you know, different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Must have been hard for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah, you have weight, and they drilled a hole, I remember, in the bed. And they put all the weight on and the-- and the ankle and the-- There are certain things you remember. But can I use the-- my-- my eye is running a little. Can I use that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's allergies or whatever. OK, that's better. It runs on one side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about how the house was furnished?

speaker: interviewee
text: The house was furnished-- let's see. In the living room was-- let's start from the bedroom. There was two beds. In the other one was one bed. And then I had a bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I usually slept on the-- what do you call it? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know where you-- where you-- where you put the-- the coals in there? The [INAUDIBLE] chuck in the kitchen--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the oven.

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it-- right. It goes through the house. And I slept-- I had a bed moved over there. And so I always stayed warm. You know, in Europe, no matter how much you were worked, you still were cold. I had everybody one time in my bed. I said, no, no. And were in the living room, we have an, a big buffet-- two. One on the bottom. One on the top one. And a table. And I think there was six chairs, I guess. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have electricity in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were the first one really. We were selling-- we were the first one to put electricity. I think it was 1937. I mean, after that time, after that time we didn't have electricity, and the town didn't have it. But they were selling it. And then, we were selling it in the store to people that wanted it. And after that time, there was lamps, you know, gasoline lamps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have indoor plumbing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not indoor plumbing. No indoor water. I remember I used to carry the water on my shoulder. Then, you go to the well, and-- and then you have barrels. And then, you dump the water. It was a job. I mean, I was young and didn't mind to do that though.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have servants in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have for me. Not for my older brother or my daughter because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only for me that they had one-- a governess.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did she teach you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She just-- all what she did is took care of me. That was her job. Well, most of the time, my mother was there. But, I mean, she didn't-- in the kitchen, she didn't-- she was cleaning and taking care of me. And that's about all she did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. But I remember she used to come even-- even she got married, she didn't have no children. She used to come and check up on me, see how I'm doing. But otherwise-- well, she helped me out during the war a little. But always she couldn't do I suppose. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Someone else who went [INAUDIBLE] really.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about Shabbos and yontif?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very observant. Like Shabbos well, Shabbos-- well, you remember, like, we had that store. And the Shabbos in-- like the night before, and he says, close the store. He used to klopf at the stechen. He had to close the door. And my father's rooms-- he says, let's take everything in there. He wasn't ready for the second time. And the Shabbos, you know, when Shabbos was, the store was closed. From Friday-- Friday till that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the whole town closed down.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the Jewish people. You could-- you're not supposed to sell on Sunday, but some customer, they come to the house. And I remember I used to stay outside, and when the policeman was not there, we'd let him in. And then later, we'd let him out. Otherwise, there was a fine for opening. No business could be open on Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father ever get fined?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you had electricity in your house, did you have a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I had a radio. I told you that-- what was that? Telefunken?

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Telefunken. And I remember the news. Everybody-- some of the neighbors were staying in the window and listening to that. Because not everybody had a radio.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a telephone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't know. Next door was some business. What was it? It was a bank. Yeah, was a bank there. And they had a telephone. Nobody had telephones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was your favorite holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: My favorite holiday-- Pesach.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they used to make all kinds of [NON-ENGLISH] and all kind of-- [NON-ENGLISH]. And I think it used to take like six, no, 46 [INAUDIBLE] and Purim and Pesach to get everything that they would take and paint everything. This was a big-- a big holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help?

speaker: interviewee
text: I helped to take down the dishes. All the dishes were in the attic. And you had to put a-- a ladder and crawl up. And like three, four people pass on all the dishes. And then after it's over, you got to put them back up. Every-- every year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what they looked like-- those dishes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know they tell me to be careful. These dishes are 100 years old. Don't drop it. So I know it sounded they were-- were leave from generation to generation. I can't recall what they look. No, I can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite thing to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: To eat. What did they make? They make like Chunt. I liked that Chunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was for Shabbos.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: With meat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they make it in the house or send it to the baker?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in the house. I mean, matter of fact, in the beginning, I remember my mother was baking even challah. And she was baking challah. But she was baking bread too. But later, a bakery come in town, and then she didn't bake no bread. But she still challah, and she used to get up, like, Friday 4 o'clock in the morning, again, have to put the yeast in that. And then all kind of thing. She used to bake for our Shabbos though.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went in [NON-ENGLISH] Tarbut, I guess. And now, I get it. And after all the years, it come to-- back to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you learn there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was secular Hebrew. But you had to have, I think, one hour or two hours in Polish and all the other one was in Hebrew. All the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, no. Later on, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Something-- I was-- I was not motivated enough. He did-- it was not-- well, let's see. How could I put-- I was bored with it. But later on, it got better.

speaker: interviewer
text: What subjects did you like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd say I liked science. Science, I liked. What I knew were they-- they were not teaching-- they were teaching, like, let's see. What were they teaching, most of it? Hebrew. And then they were teaching, like, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what you call it from the-- what do you call it in the [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn Chumash and Tanakh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chumash, yeah. And Tanakh.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about Talmud Did you learn--

speaker: interviewee
text: Talmud.

speaker: interviewer
text: With mefarshim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: With commentators?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, no, without commentators.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not even Rashi?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Rashi? No Rashi?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is Rashi? I'm forgetting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rashi was a French rabbi. He made commentaries on the Bible. Did you have a lot of sports in your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, soccer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you good?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was too chubby. I couldn't run after the ball. [LAUGHING] But I still played though. I had fun, and I played.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: My bar mitzvah, yeah. I think I'm born in 15 day at Thomas. But now, I forget that what-- what Haftarah is. I forgot. I should have called the rabbi and asked him what Haftarah was. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were called to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the bimah. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't do the parasha. You just did the Haftarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the Haftarah. Well, there they don't do it like here.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do they do it there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They do it like-- oop. They do-- they do the Haftarah. They, I mean, you're 13 here, they don't let you to the bimah. There's special people for that. It's not like over here. When you're 13 years, you can pray. You can go on the bimah. Over there, you're a good bar mitzvah, you say Haftarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then did you have a big party after it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In shul, you have to bring all kind of-- in shul, make a party. And then in the afternoon, another party at home. You know, the neighbors. This was strictly for the temple, when I was there. See I remember little thing-- the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the temple? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it. Don't remember. I talked to my brother, and we sat down both and don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the presents you got for your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a little stuff, not too much really, you know. People were-- most of the people that come to a bar mitzvah, they-- they cooked and that. And everybody bring things. And-- But I remember the bar mitzvah lasted from-- from noon all the way till the evening. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember that's about-- what I know? I don't remember all of that. I don't know. Your friends-- were your friends were all Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: --boys like you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only they didn't-- in school we didn't wear no-- no kippahs. Just--

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were all Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish. I didn't have any Gentile friend. A Gentle friend, my only memory-- I know it weren't-- the shoemaker was Gentile. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: In your normal life, did Jews and Gentiles-- did they mix at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They only-- they only used to come and buy. And I think every Thursday was like a farmers' market.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any problems with anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: I personally didn't have much because we stuck all together in the same group.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents either because they were-- had their own business. Later, through the years, it got worse though.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what was the first time when you heard the name Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- when was it-- in 1935, and I-- that's when I could hear it already.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was before your bar mitzvah or around the time when you were getting ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hear that he's-- he's rounding up Jewish people and sending them out from the-- in the German news because at that time, Poland was not occupied.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear this on the radio? How did you hear it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was some commentator from England. From England, commentators through the radio. BB, what is it?

speaker: interviewer
text: BBC?

speaker: interviewee
text: BBC?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did somebody speak English in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there's-- they were talking in some other [INAUDIBLE] in a different language where they could-- BBC usually broadcast in all kind of languages so they could pick it up in their language what they knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you heard this, what did you think? Or, first of all, did people believe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was-- when I was young, I was really scared. I was a kid. And, yes, the parents, and they calm you down. Ah, that's nothing. And you know, you just-- and you hear all of the bad things, and you're a little kid. You're kind of afraid about it. But they calm you down, the parents. Oh, he dare not come in here. It's only there. You know how things are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I drink a little water? I got to have permission, you know, Ari. And when did you start to take it seriously?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I started taking it seriously, in '39. In '39 when I could-- win the-- and the-- and this, the Russian took over one part. And then the refugees from-- from Poland start to come in to us, and we have to take in-- every family had to take in two or three people, I think. Matter of fact, we took in a couple-- people from the other side of Poland, you know.

words.text: to us, and we have to

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they said they are-- they are killing people, and they're putting them in jail. And that they can raid our businesses. And it was no good though. But still, people didn't believe it.
